title: - WHAT TO PRUNE AND WHAT NOT TO PRUNE AT INI TIALIZATION [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - WHAT TO PRUNE AND WHAT NOT TO PRUNE AT INI TIALIZATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Post-training dropout based approaches achieve high sparsity and are well established means of deciphering problems relating to computational cost and overfitting in Neural Network architectures citesrivastava2014dropout, (Pan et al., 2016), Zhu & Gupta (2017), LeCun et al. (1990). Contrastingly, pruning at initialization is still far behind Frankle et al. (2020). Initialization pruning is more efficacious when it comes to scaling computation cost of the network. Furthermore, it handles overfitting just as well as post training dropout. It is also averse to retraining losses.

In approbation of the above reasons, the paper presents two approaches to prune at initialization. The goal is to achieve higher sparsity while preserving performance. 1) K-starts, begins with k random p-sparse matrices at initialization. In the first couple of epochs the network then determines the ”fittest” of these p-sparse matrices in an attempt to find the ”lottery ticket” Frankle & Carbin (2018) p-sparse network. The approach is adopted from how evolutionary algorithms find the best individual. Depending on the Neural Network architecture, fitness criteria can be based on magnitude of network weights, magnitude of gradient accumulation over an epoch or a combination of both. 2) Dissipating gradients approach, aims at eliminating weights that remain within a fraction of their initial value during the first couple of epochs. Removing weights in this manner despite their magnitude best preserves performance of the network. Contrarily, the approach also takes the most epochs to achieve higher sparsity. 3) Combination of dissipating gradients and kstarts outperforms either methods and random dropout consistently.

The benefits of using the provided pertaining approaches are: 1) They do not require specific knowledge of the classification task, fixing of dropout threshold or regularization parameters 2) Retraining of the model is neither necessary nor affects the performance of the p-sparse network.
We evaluate the efficacy of the said methods on Autoencoders and Fully Connected Multilayered Perceptrons. The datasets used are MNIST and Fashion MNIST.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Computational complexity and overfitting in neural networks is a well established problem Frankle & Carbin (2018), Han et al. (2015), LeCun et al. (1990), Denil et al. (2013). We utilize pruning approaches for the following two reasons: 1) To reduce the computational cost of a fully connected neural network. 2) To reduce overfitting in the network.

Given a large number of post-training pruning approaches Srivastava et al. (2014), Geman et al. (1992), Pan et al. (2016), the paper attempts to propose two pre-training pruning approaches: kstarts and dissipating gradients. Moreover, it appears to be the case that when isolated from other factors sparse networks outperform fully connected networks. When not isolated they perform at least as well up to a percentage of sparsity depending on the number of parameters in the said network. kstarts and dissipating gradients provide are simple nevertheless effective methods to quickly look for best sparse networks to.
The approaches exploit the knowledge that a network has multiple underlying p-sparse networks that perform just as well and in some cases even better when contrasted with their fully connected

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

counterparts Frankle & Carbin (2018). What percentage of sparsity is realized, depends largely on the number of parameters originally present in the network. Such sparse networks are potent in preventing over-fitting and reducing computational cost.

The poset-training pruning has several approaches in place such as adding various regularization schemes to prune the network Louizos et al. (2017), Pan et al. (2016) or using second derivative or hessian of the weights for dropout LeCun et al. (1990), Hassibi & Stork (1993). Han et al. (2015), Alford et al. (2019), Zhu & Gupta (2017) use an efficient iterative pruning method to iteratively increase sparsity. Srivastava et al. (2014) dropout random hidden units with p probability instead of weights to avoid overfitting in general. Each of these approaches is effective and achieves good sparsity post-training.

We use a simple intuitive models that achieve good results and exploits the fact that a number of sub networks in a Neural Network has the potential to individually learn the input Srivastava et al. (2014). We decide on a sparse network early on based on the dropout method and use only that for training. This provides an edge for faster computation, quicker elimination of excess weights and reduced generalization error. The sparsity achieved is superior to random dropout.

Section II gives a general introduction to all the methods, section III defines p-sparsity, section IV provides the algorithm for both approaches, section V describes experimental setup and results, section VI discusses various design choices, section VII gives a general discussion of results, section VIII discusses limitations of the approach and section IX provides conclusions and final remarks.

## 2 PRUNING METHODS

## 2.1 KSTARTS

## 2.1.1 KSTARTS AND EVOLUTIONARY ALGORITHMS

We take the concept of k random starts from Evolutionary Algorithms (Vikhar, 2016) that use a fitness function or heuristic to perform ”natural selection” in optimization and search based problems (Goldberg & Holland, 1988). It is relatively simple to fit genetic algorithms to the problem at hand. Other method that would be equally effective with a little bit of modification are Hunting Search (Oftadeh et al., 2010), Natural Adaptation Strategies (Wierstra et al., 2008), firefly algorithm (Yang, 2010) etc.

The basic components of the algorithm are: (1) **Population** : A product of network weights and sparse matrices. (2) **Individual** : An instance of the population. (3) **Fitness Function** : The heuristic chosen for evaluation of the population.

## 2.1.2 POPULATION

We first initialize K sparse matrices, a single instance of these K sparse matrices can be seen in equation **??** . In every iteration we multiply model weights W of the Network layer in question with every instance of the K sparse matrices. The resulting set of matrices is our population for that iteration. Each iteration is referred to as a new generation.

_population_ = _W ∗ K − SparseMatrices_

(1)

## 2.1.3 INDIVIDUAL

Each individual, in a population of K instances, is a sparse matrix of size equal to the size of network weights, W. The number of 0’s and 1’s in the sparsity matrix are determined by the connectivity factor p which is further described in section 3. An sparse matrix of _p ≈_ 0 _._ 5 will have _≈_ 50% 0’s and _≈_ 50% 1s.

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## 2.1.4 EVALUATION/FITNESS FUNCTION

The fitness of an individual is ranked by determining the sum of each individual in population as given in 1 such that the fittest individual in a generation is given by equation 2.

where _i ∗ c_ is the size of each individual and ind refers to the individual in population.

## 2.1.5 NEXT GENERATION SELECTION
Assuming each iteration is the next generation. In each generation the fit individual is favoured so:

- The fittest individual is passed of as weight to the next generation.

- Every 5 generations or as per the decided elimination frequency, the individual with lowest fitness is discarded from the population.

## 2.2 DISSIPATING GRADIENTS

Magnitude and gradient based pruning approaches are popular in post-training pruning Srivastava et al. (2014), LeCun et al. (1990). It does not make much sense to employ them pre-training becauseof the randomly generated weights. But in order to reduce error, any network aims to target updating weights that influence the results most. Based upon that hypothesis, in each epoch we sum gradients over and eliminate weights whose weights are not getting updated. In equation 3 N is the total number of iterations for an epoch. In equation 4 epsilon is 1e-6 for all experiments.

One consideration in this approach is to not do this for too many epochs which can be only 2 if the image is very monochrome and more than 2 if the gradients are dissipating more slowly. Moreover, once specific weights have reached their optimal learning, their gradients will dissipate and we don’t want to eliminate them.

## 2.3 COMBINATION DROPOUT

Combination dropout is merely combining Kstarts with dissipating gradients. The weights eliminated use both approaches. We fix p for Kstarts to a certain value of minimum sparsity and further eliminate weights that dissipating gradients method will eliminate as well. The approach achieves better performance than either methods.

## 3 DEFINING P-SPARSITY

In a p-sparse network layer approximately p percent of connection between two layers are eliminated. Figure 1 shows a fully connected conventional neural network (figure 1a) and three sparsely connected networks with different values of p (figures 1b, 1c, 1d).

## 3.1 CONNECTIVITY FACTOR

Connectivity factor, p, determines the percentage of connections to be removed between any two layers of the network. For instance if p = 0.0 than the network is be fully connected as shown in figure 1a, if on the opposite extreme, p=1.0, then there will be no connections between two layers of neurons. If p=0.5, figure 1b, only approximately 50% of the connection in the network remain. If p=0.3, figure 1c, approximately 70% of the connection still exist and if p=0.7, figure 1d, a mere 30% of the connections are active between two network layers. In short p determines percentage of 0’s in an individual sparse matrix shown in equation **??** .

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

(a) A fully connected (b) A sample 0.5- (c) A sample 0.3- (d) A sample 0.7-
or 0.0-Sparse network Sparse network layer Sparse network layer Sparse network layer
layer
**----- End of picture text -----**

Figure 1: Sample P-Sparse Network Layers

## 4 ALGORITHM

The autoencoder, two and three layered neural networks are trained in a standard manner with adam optimizer and batch update.

## 4.1 KSTARTS ALGORITHM

Algorithm 1 gives the kstarts method:

1. We have K number of sparse matrices as shown in equation **??** generated and we call them KI in algorithm 1.

2. Every time the weight W needs to be updated instead of the usual gradient update we add one step further and using the fitness function, pass the fittest individual as the new W which biases the network towards that sparse matrix.

3. Every 5 iterations, the individual with lowest fitness is dropped.

**Algorithm 1:** k Random starts

**input :** Data, params,K,p **output:** W,b initialize W,b; _KI ←_ K individuals with approximately 1-p percent active connections; **for** _maxiterations_ **do** Run Neural Network; Update weights; **if** _One individual in KI is left_ **then** W _←_ individual; **else** W _←_ individual with maximum sum (of weights); **for** _every 5 iterations_ **do** pop individual with minimum sum (of weights) from KI; **end end end**

- 4.2 DISSIPATING GRADIENTS ALGORITHM

Algorithm 2 is more simple and just eliminates weights with sum of gradients equal to zero in the first 1-4 epochs depending on the desired sparsity.

Figure 2: Performance of dropout methods with increasing sparsity.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS AND RESULTS

The experiments performed on two datasets; MNIST Deng (2012) and Fashion MNIST Xiao et al. (2017). The network architectures are two layered Autoencoder (784-128-64), a three layered NN (784-128-100-10) both with sigmoid activation and adam optimization functions.

The Architecture used for learning curves is a single layered NN(784-10).

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

**----- Start of picture text -----**

(a) Dataset : Fashion MNIST (b) Dataset : MNIST
**----- End of picture text -----**

Figure 3: Effect of sample size on learning when varying the sparsity of the network with kstarts. Each trial involves running a p-sparse network on a fixed training set of N examples from dataset for 2.5k iterations and is averaged over 10 trials each.

## 5.1 EFFECT OF INCREASING SPARSITY

As sparsity increases, overall performance reduces. Figure 2 shows the behaviour of various dropout methods presented in this paper.

In case of random dropout, it’s indeed a random shot. Either no useful weight is eliminated or multiple crucial weights are eliminated which decides how well does random dropout perform. Kstarts performs slightly better on average with multiple start choices. Depending on how many independent p-sparse networks are present in the network that can learn well, one of them can be identified, given k is large enough and the fitness function is smartly decided by first examining the weights and gradients.

Dissipating gradients works well as long as the network isn’t learning very fast i.e. some weights are being updated in the consequent epochs. It’s also most reliable. Combination works by far the best because it does not only rely on eliminating weights that are not being updated but also uses kstarts. It seems to achieve superior performance as long as p value chosen is a value that kstarts performs well on.

## 5.2 VARIATION IN SAMPLE SIZE

Figures 3a and 3b show relationship of varying sample size to different sparsity values in a single layer NN over 2.5k iterations.

The interesting result here is that isolated from all other factors like number of parameters, hidden units and various design choices, kstarts dropout performs better on a single layer network compared to even a fully connected network. The standard deviation is also lower to fully connected network as well as random dropout. kstarts dropout also learns faster than a fully connected network. For instance, if the iterations for the experiments in figure 3a and 3b are increased, the fully connected network will eventually reach accuracy of the p-sparse network.

## 6 DESIGN PARAMETER CHOICES

There are a number of design parameter choices for the algorithm 1 presented here. Some are explained in detail in this section.

## 6.1 CHOICE OF FITNESS FUNCTION

Since the fitness function determines the individual being passed on to the next generation and the individual being eliminated. We had three choices for choosing the fitness of an individual each with it’s own pros and cons.

6

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

- **Magnitude:** As opted here, we choose population as shown in equation 1 and then select fitness using equation 2. This skews the selection of new weights to the previously selected sparse matrix from KI and therefore, the initial sparse matrix will be propagated forward. This also renders elimination to be pointless it does not matter if 1 or all other matrices in K are eliminated. Furthermore, the sparse matrix is picked awfully early in the experiments i.e. only after first 5 iterations or so and that is not when weights have reached a saturation point.
- **Gradient:** The second choice is to use gradient of the weights to create population as follows:

- _population_ = _δW ∗ SparseMatrices_ (5)

- doing so can have fitness totally dependant on the current update and the new weights are heavily skewed towards the performance of the current iterations which again doesn’t seem that appropriate.
- **Sum of Gradients:** The third option is summing up the gradients for a number of iterations in the manner we do for dissipating gradients 3 and then use those to create population:

Doing so skews the network toward weights that are updated quickly and are increasing.

## 6.2 NO. OF LAYERS AND NO. HIDDEN UNITS

We initially use single layer NN (784-10) to isolate the effects of kstarts algorithm from effects of other parameters that may have a large impact on performance as the size of the network grows. Those parameters i.e. number of hidden layers, regularization choices, types of layers may aid or adversely effect the performance of the algorithm and by performing the experiments on the basic unit of a neural network we were able to concur that the method is effective in it’s own right. After concluding the approach works we tested on three layered NN and Autoencoder.

## 6.3 COST EFFECTIVESNESS

One beneficial feature of pre-training pruning approaches is that the best p-sparse network is quickly identified. There are a number of methods that exploit sparsity of a matrix for quicker multiplication i.e. (Yuster & Zwick, 2005), (Buluc¸ & Gilbert, 2012) which can be used to quickly retrain large networks. Although that is out of scope for our findings.

## 7 DISCUSSION

- 7.1 EFFECT OF K

1. From the experiments done so far lower K ( _≈_ 10) outperforms or at least performs as well as a higher value of K ( _≈_ 100). This can be because the more times a different matrix is chosen by the network, the more times the network has to adopt to learning with only that p-sparse network.

## 7.2 EFFECT OF P

1. A sparse network can outperforms a fully connected network for lower number of iterations and smaller networks.

2. An appropriate value of p, for instance in these experiments _p ≈_ 0 _._ 5, seems to work best for random dropout, kstarts dropout and combination dropout. A poor choice of p can’t seem to be remedied by a better choice of K.

3. p can be thought of as an information limiter. For better learning, if the network is only provided with particular features, it might have an easier time learning the class specific features in different nodes but this only remains a wishful speculation and requires further analysis. Table 1 shows relationship between k,p and no of iterations in a single layer NN (784-10)

7

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

Table 1: MNIST-Mean Accuracy and standard deviation averaged over 10 runs

## 8 LIMITATIONS

There are a number of limitations to the approach and further investigation is required in a number of domains.
1. The NNs used are single and three layered feed forward networks and autoencoders. CNNs are not experimented upon.
2. Only classification tasks are considered and those on only two datasets: MNIST and Fashion MNIST.
3. Ideally using sparse matrix should make for efficient computation but since the algorithms for that are not used it at this point does not show what the time comparison of the approaches will be.

## 9 CONCLUSIONS AND FINAL REMARKS

We present two methods for pruning weights pre-training or in the first couple of epochs. The comparisons are made against random dropout and both approaches mostly perform better than the random dropout. We provide a combination dropout approach that consistently outperforms other dropout approaches. A lot more analysis of the approach is required on multiple datasets, learning tasks and network architectures but the basic methods seem to be effective.

## REFERENCES

This document was extracted by OCR engine and could contain mistakes.

zero_shot__claude-opus-4.7.label: Zero Shot (claude-opus-4.7)
zero_shot__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: The paper proposes two pre-training pruning methods (kstarts and dissipating gradients) and a combination of them, evaluated on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST with MLPs and autoencoders. While the core ideas are intuitive and the empirical results modestly support the claims, the paper suffers from significant issues: broken equation references (multiple '??' placeholders), missing/unshown key equations (the sparsity matrix equation, fitness function equation 2, and gradient accumulation equation 3 are referenced but never actually displayed in usable form), vague algorithmic descriptions that hinder reproducibility, and sweeping claims that are not adequately supported by the limited experiments. The writing has numerous grammatical issues and the experimental scope (two simple datasets, small MLPs/autoencoders) is too narrow to support the general claims made. The comparison baseline is only 'random dropout' — not any of the well-known pruning-at-initialization methods (SNIP, GraSP, etc.) that would be the natural comparisons given the paper's positioning.

comments.title: Broken equation references throughout the paper
comments.quote: a single instance of these K sparse matrices can be seen in equation **??**
comments.explanation: Multiple equation references in the paper are rendered as '??' (e.g., in Section 2.1.2, Section 3.1, and Section 4.1). These are not OCR errors — they are unresolved LaTeX \ref{} tags pointing to labels that do not exist in the compiled document. This means the key definition of the 'sparse matrix' that kstarts uses as its fundamental object is never actually shown. A reader cannot determine what the sparse matrix looks like beyond the vague description 'matrix of 0's and 1's.' This is a reproducibility-affecting gap.

comments.title: Fitness function equation 2 is referenced but not displayed
comments.quote: The fitness of an individual is ranked by determining the sum of each individual in population as given in 1 such that the fittest individual in a generation is given by equation 2.
comments.explanation: The text promises an equation 2 that defines the fittest individual, and states 'where i*c is the size of each individual and ind refers to the individual in population' — but no actual formula is rendered between these lines. Only the phrase appears with variables (i*c, ind) that are never shown in a formula. This is the central selection criterion of the kstarts algorithm and its absence prevents understanding the method. Combined with the '??' references, the formal specification of kstarts is essentially missing.

comments.title: Equations 3 and 4 referenced but not shown
comments.quote: In equation 3 N is the total number of iterations for an epoch. In equation 4 epsilon is 1e-6 for all experiments.
comments.explanation: The text for the dissipating gradients method refers to equations 3 and 4 — presumably defining the accumulated gradient and the thresholding — but neither is actually displayed in the rendered text. Without these, the method can only be inferred from the (also informally specified) Algorithm 2. Notably, Algorithm 2 uses threshold '0.0001' but the text says epsilon is '1e-6'. This is an inconsistency between the prose/missing equation and the algorithm listing that undermines the specification of the method.

comments.title: Inconsistent threshold value between text and Algorithm 2
comments.quote: if  accumulated d W <  0 . 0001  then
comments.explanation: Algorithm 2 uses a threshold of 0.0001 on the accumulated gradient to decide elimination, but the surrounding text states 'In equation 4 epsilon is 1e-6 for all experiments.' These values differ by two orders of magnitude. Without seeing the actual equation 4, a reader cannot know which threshold was used in the reported experiments. This is a concrete inconsistency that affects reproducibility.

comments.title: Fitness-by-sum-of-weights is a questionable selection heuristic
comments.quote: W _←_ individual with maximum sum (of weights);
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1 the fittest individual is chosen as the one with maximum sum of weights (population = W * sparse matrix, then sum). Because weights can be negative, the individual with 'maximum sum' may simply be the one that happens to mask out negative weights — not a principled fitness criterion related to performance. The authors acknowledge in Section 6.1 that magnitude-based fitness 'skews the selection… to the previously selected sparse matrix' and is chosen 'awfully early,' but do not resolve this with an alternative; they just list alternatives. This leaves the core algorithmic choice underjustified. It would be more natural to use the sum of absolute values or L2 norm.

comments.title: Claim about 'lottery ticket' is overstated
comments.quote: in an attempt to find the "lottery ticket" Frankle & Carbin (2018) p-sparse network
comments.explanation: The paper invokes the lottery ticket framing but its method simply selects among K random sparse masks based on early-training statistics. Frankle & Carbin's lottery ticket hypothesis specifically involves identifying a sub-network that can be trained from the original initialization to match full-model performance — it is not merely 'any good sparse mask.' The paper does not show that the selected sub-network, trained from its original initialization, matches the dense baseline; in fact Figure 2 appears to show performance degrading with sparsity. Framing this as finding the lottery ticket overstates what is demonstrated.

comments.title: Claim that sparse networks outperform fully connected is not adequately supported
comments.quote: kstarts dropout performs better on a single layer network compared to even a fully connected network
comments.explanation: This claim about Figure 3 is made based on a single-layer NN (784-10) at 2.5k iterations on MNIST/Fashion-MNIST. The authors themselves note in the next sentence 'if the iterations … are increased, the fully connected network will eventually reach accuracy of the p-sparse network.' So the 'outperformance' is purely a transient effect during early training, not a genuine advantage of sparsity. Stating 'kstarts dropout performs better' without this qualifier in the same breath is misleading — especially since the abstract and introduction both suggest sparse networks outperform dense ones 'when isolated from other factors.'

comments.title: Missing comparison to established pruning-at-initialization methods
comments.quote: The comparisons are made against random dropout and both approaches mostly perform better than the random dropout.
comments.explanation: The paper frames itself as advancing pruning at initialization and cites Frankle et al. 2020 ('Pruning neural networks at initialization: Why are we missing the mark?'), which surveys methods like SNIP, GraSP, and SynFlow. Yet the only baseline is random dropout. Beating random dropout is a very weak bar for a pruning-at-initialization method and does not support the paper's positioning. This is not a mathematical error but a methodological gap that undermines the paper's central contribution claim.

comments.title: Table 1 column labels show 'p=0.5' twice and do not match header
comments.explanation: The Table 1 header shows p=0.0 and then 'p=0.5' listed twice (once presumably a section header and once perhaps redundantly), with four columns below it labeled k=1, k=10, k=50, k=100. The first data column under p=0.0 has only one value per row, so the structure implies: one column for p=0.0 (dense baseline, k irrelevant) and four columns for p=0.5 at different k. The duplicated 'p=0.5' label likely should be a single spanning header. While this is a presentation issue, it also makes the table hard to read and could mislead a careful reader about which numbers correspond to which setting.

comments.title: Dissipating gradients algorithm: final multiplication step is ambiguous
comments.quote: W  ← W*accumulated dW;
comments.explanation: At the end of each epoch, the algorithm multiplies W by 'accumulated_dW' where accumulated_dW has been set to either 0 or 1 based on the threshold. This is effectively a mask application. However, the description 'if accumulated_dW < 0.0001 then accumulated_dW ← 0; else accumulated_dW ← 1' compares a (presumably per-weight) tensor to a scalar threshold — the algorithm is elementwise but this is not stated, and the order (first check, then set to 0/1) is a thresholding operation that should be more clearly specified. Moreover, once a weight is zeroed out in epoch t, its gradient will remain ~0 in subsequent accumulations, so the mask can only monotonically increase in sparsity — this property is not discussed, nor is how 'once specific weights have reached their optimal learning, their gradients will dissipate and we don't want to eliminate them' is actually handled by the algorithm (the algorithm appears to eliminate them regardless).

comments.title: Algorithm 1 elimination logic is underspecified
comments.quote: if  One individual in KI is left  then W _←_ individual; else W _←_ individual with maximum sum (of weights); for  every 5 iterations  do pop individual with minimum sum (of weights) from KI;
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 passes 'the fittest individual as the new W' each iteration (text Section 2.1.5), but the loop structure has both 'W ← individual with maximum sum' (every iteration) and 'pop individual with minimum sum from KI' (every 5 iterations). If W is overwritten every iteration by the current best sparse × W product, what remains for the gradient update in the next iteration? Is the mask fixed for 5 iterations and only the dense W updated, or does W itself become this masked product each step (effectively changing the trainable parameters each step)? The phrase 'the fittest individual is passed of as weight to the next generation' suggests the former, but the algorithm listing suggests W is literally overwritten each iteration. This ambiguity makes the method hard to reproduce.

comments.title: Unsupported claim that initialization pruning handles overfitting 'just as well as' post-training dropout
comments.quote: Furthermore, it handles overfitting just as well as post training dropout.
comments.explanation: This claim appears in the abstract but no experiment in the paper directly compares generalization gap or overfitting metrics between initialization pruning and post-training dropout. Training/test accuracy curves as a function of sparsity are shown (Figure 2) but not framed as an overfitting comparison, and post-training dropout (Srivastava et al.) is not actually a baseline in any experiment. The claim therefore overstates what the paper demonstrates.

zero_shot__gpt-5.5.label: Zero Shot (gpt-5.5)
zero_shot__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper proposes intuitive initialization-time pruning heuristics, and the high-level motivation is clear: choose a sparse subnetwork early rather than pruning after full training. However, several core methodological details are either internally inconsistent or mathematically problematic. The most serious concerns are with the actual K-starts update rule, which appears to collapse the population almost immediately and makes later mask selection/elimination ineffective, and with the dissipating-gradients rule, which thresholds signed accumulated gradients rather than gradient magnitudes or actual parameter movement. There are also contradictions between the claimed lack of thresholds and the explicit use of sparsity/gradient thresholds, and the experiments are not specified in enough detail to reproduce the reported comparisons. Overall, the ideas are potentially interesting, but the current presentation does not yet provide a technically reliable or reproducible method.

comments.title: K-starts update appears to destroy the population structure it relies on
comments.quote: **Magnitude:** As opted here, we choose population as shown in equation 1 and then select fitness using equation 2. This skews the selection of new weights to the previously selected sparse matrix from KI and therefore, the initial sparse matrix will be propagated forward. This also renders elimination to be pointless it does not matter if 1 or all other matrices in K are eliminated.
comments.explanation: I initially read K-starts as maintaining K candidate masks and repeatedly comparing their performance or saliency before committing to one sparse subnetwork. That would be a plausible initialization-pruning heuristic. However, the algorithm says that after each update the weights are replaced by the currently fittest sparse individual, i.e. a masked version of W. Once W itself has zeros outside the selected mask, future products W times other masks cannot recover those zeroed connections. The quoted discussion in Section 6.1 actually recognizes this problem: with the magnitude fitness used in the experiments, the initially selected sparse matrix is propagated and later elimination is effectively pointless. This is not merely a minor implementation detail; it undermines the claimed evolutionary/K-starts search over sparse subnetworks, because the method seems to collapse to choosing one mask very early rather than evaluating K alternatives over generations.

comments.title: Fitness is described as a raw sum of weights rather than a magnitude or performance criterion
comments.quote: The fitness of an individual is ranked by determining the sum of each individual in population as given in 1 such that the fittest individual in a generation is given by equation 2.
comments.explanation: The authors' intent seems to be to use a simple saliency proxy for choosing among sparse masks, and later Section 6.1 labels this option as “Magnitude.” But the formal/procedural description repeatedly says the fitness is the sum of weights, not the sum of absolute weights, squared weights, gradient-weight products, loss change, or validation performance. For symmetrically initialized neural-network weights, the signed sum is not a standard measure of weight importance: positive and negative entries can cancel, and masks containing more positive weights can be favored for arbitrary sign reasons. If the intended criterion is magnitude, the formula/procedure needs absolute values, e.g. \(\sum |W \odot M_k|\), or another clearly defined norm. As written, the selection criterion is inconsistent with the “magnitude” interpretation and is not justified as a meaningful fitness function.

comments.title: Dissipating-gradients pruning thresholds signed accumulated gradients, causing cancellation and sign bias
comments.explanation: The stated goal is to remove weights whose gradients have dissipated, i.e. weights that are not being meaningfully updated. I checked whether the algorithm uses a magnitude, norm, or absolute accumulated update, but the pseudocode compares the signed accumulated gradient directly to a positive threshold. This is technically problematic. First, positive and negative gradients over an epoch can cancel even when the weight is receiving large updates, so the sum being near zero does not imply inactivity. Second, any sufficiently negative accumulated gradient is automatically less than 0.0001 and would be pruned, even if its magnitude is large. Standard pruning criteria based on gradients would normally use \(|\sum_t g_t|\), \(\sum_t |g_t|\), a norm, or an estimated loss effect, depending on the intended notion of saliency. The current rule does not faithfully implement “not getting updated” and could systematically remove actively learning weights.

comments.title: Gradient threshold value is inconsistent between text and algorithm
comments.quote: In equation 4 epsilon is 1e-6 for all experiments.
comments.explanation: The text specifies \(\epsilon = 10^{-6}\) for all experiments, but Algorithm 2 thresholds accumulated gradients at 0.0001, i.e. \(10^{-4}\). I considered whether one of these might be an OCR artifact, but both values are plausible-looking numerical constants and differ by two orders of magnitude. For a pruning rule whose sparsity depends directly on the threshold, this is not a harmless discrepancy. A reader cannot know which threshold generated the reported results, and the difference could materially affect the number of weights pruned.

comments.title: Dissipating-gradients description alternates between movement from initialization and gradient accumulation
comments.quote: Dissipating gradients approach, aims at eliminating weights that remain within a fraction of their initial value during the first couple of epochs.
comments.explanation: This sentence suggests a criterion based on parameter displacement from initialization, for example pruning weights whose \(|W_t - W_0|\) remains below some fraction of \(|W_0|\). That would be a coherent, though still heuristic, initialization-time pruning idea. But the later method and pseudocode prune based on accumulated gradients, not on the distance from the initial value, and no fraction of the initial value is actually defined. These criteria are related only under additional assumptions about optimizer dynamics and signed gradient accumulation, especially because Adam updates are not simply proportional to raw accumulated gradients. The prose therefore describes a different pruning rule from the one implemented.

comments.title: Claim of not requiring threshold choices conflicts with the defined sparsity and pruning thresholds
comments.quote: They do not require specific knowledge of the classification task, fixing of dropout threshold or regularization parameters
comments.explanation: I understand the authors' intended contrast with methods that tune regularization strengths or post-training pruning schedules. However, the proposed methods still require several threshold-like choices: the connectivity/sparsity parameter \(p\), the number of K-starts, the elimination frequency, the number of early epochs for dissipating gradients, and the gradient threshold \(\epsilon\) or 0.0001 in Algorithm 2. Section 7.2 also states that a poor choice of \(p\) cannot be remedied by K, which confirms that this parameter is important. Thus the method may avoid some conventional regularization parameters, but it does not avoid fixing pruning/dropout thresholds in the ordinary sense.

comments.title: Experimental setup is underspecified for reproducing the reported comparisons
comments.quote:
The experiments performed on two datasets; MNIST Deng (2012) and Fashion MNIST Xiao et al. (2017). The network architectures are two layered Autoencoder (784-128-64), a three layered NN (784-128-100-10) both with sigmoid activation and adam optimization functions.

The Architecture used for learning curves is a single layered NN(784-10).
comments.explanation: The paper gives the datasets, broad architectures, activation, and optimizer, which is a useful starting point. But several reproduction-critical choices are missing or unclear: Adam learning rate and beta parameters, batch size, loss functions, preprocessing/normalization, train/test split usage, number of epochs or iterations for each figure, exact \(p\) and \(K\) values for each plotted curve, whether biases are pruned, how masks are sampled per layer, whether masks are fixed across runs, and how the random-dropout baseline is implemented. For autoencoders, the reported performance metric is also not specified in the shown text. Because the empirical claims are central to the paper, these omissions make it difficult for a reader to verify or reproduce the results.

comments.title: The claim that K-starts is isolated from other factors and outperforms fully connected networks is stronger than the evidence supports
comments.quote: The interesting result here is that isolated from all other factors like number of parameters, hidden units and various design choices, kstarts dropout performs better on a single layer network compared to even a fully connected network. The standard deviation is also lower to fully connected network as well as random dropout. kstarts dropout also learns faster than a fully connected network.
comments.explanation: The learning-curve observation may be real for the displayed MNIST/Fashion-MNIST single-layer experiments, and the authors appropriately note elsewhere that the fully connected model can eventually catch up. Still, saying the effect is “isolated from all other factors” is too strong. Changing sparsity changes the effective parameterization, optimization trajectory, implicit regularization, and possibly learning-rate sensitivity. The results appear to be from a narrow set of datasets and architectures, and the table suggests that the advantage can be small by longer training. A more defensible statement would be that, under the tested setup and fixed training budget, K-starts sometimes reaches higher accuracy faster than the dense baseline.

comments.title: Computational-cost benefit is asserted without being demonstrated in the implemented experiments
comments.quote: This provides an edge for faster computation, quicker elimination of excess weights and reduced generalization error.
comments.explanation: Pruning at initialization can in principle reduce computation if the implementation uses sparse kernels or otherwise skips pruned connections, so the motivation is reasonable. However, the paper later states that sparse-matrix algorithms are out of scope and that the current experiments do not show time comparisons. Without measured wall-clock time, FLOPs, memory access analysis, or an implementation that exploits sparsity, the faster-computation claim has not been demonstrated. The paper can claim a potential reduction in parameter count or theoretical operations, but the empirical computation-speed claim should be qualified.
